frameworks.name: Relationship Capital Architecture
frameworks.description: Networks are not collections of contacts — they are stores of relationship capital. Capital is built through consistent value delivery and depleted through extraction. Architecture determines long-term network value.
steps: Map current network by tier: Tier 1 (10-20 deep relationships — real mutual investment), Tier 2 (50-150 warm relationships — periodic meaningful contact), Tier 3 (500+ loose connections — aware of each other)
Audit Tier 1: are these the right people for your 10-year trajectory? Are you investing in them?
Identify network gaps: what expertise, access, or perspective is missing from your Tier 1?
Define your value proposition: what do you consistently offer that makes people want you in their Tier 1?
Build toward 3-5 new Tier 1 relationships per year — quality over volume
Maintain Tier 2 with systematic low-friction touchpoints — sharing relevant content, congratulations, introductions
antipatterns: Transactional networking — connecting only when you need something depletes capital rapidly
Optimizing for Tier 3 (followers, LinkedIn connections) while neglecting Tier 1
No defined value proposition — taking more than you give produces weak relationships
Networking events without follow-up — weak ties never convert to relationship capital

frameworks.name: Give First Protocol
frameworks.description: Relationship capital is built by giving before asking. Consistent givers build disproportionately strong networks because they are rare. The sequence is: give → trust builds → ask becomes natural.
steps: Before any interaction ask: what can I give this person? (Information, introduction, recognition, time, resources)
Give without expectation of immediate return — capital builds in aggregate, not per transaction
Specialize your giving: what can you offer that others cannot? (Your specific expertise, your specific network access)
Make introductions liberally — connecting two people who should know each other costs you nothing and builds capital with both
Follow up on things people mention — remembering details and acting on them is rare and memorable
Publish your knowledge — giving at scale without linear time cost
antipatterns: Keeping score on individual interactions — relationship capital is portfolio-level, not transaction-level
Generic giving — forwarding articles anyone could find is low value; specific, personalized giving is high value
Giving without visibility — value delivered that is not perceived does not build capital

frameworks.name: Strategic Network Positioning
frameworks.description: Where you sit in a network determines what information and opportunities reach you. Structural position matters as much as relationship quality.
steps: Identify the key nodes in your field: who do the most influential people in your domain all know?
Identify bridge opportunities: where are two valuable but disconnected communities you could bridge?
Position yourself at information intersections: people who connect different worlds receive more diverse, earlier-arriving information
Build weak ties deliberately: strong ties share redundant information; weak ties provide novel information and opportunities
Become a connector: facilitate introductions between your Tier 1 — connectors become structurally irreplaceable
Audit your network diversity: same-industry, same-background networks produce echo chambers and limited opportunity
antipatterns: Only building within your current industry — limits opportunity discovery and creates echo chamber
No weak ties — strong-tie-only networks are warm but informationally redundant
Never making introductions — connectors are structurally central; non-connectors are peripheral

prompts.name: Network Audit
prompts.template: Audit my network. Tier 1 (deep, mutual investment): [list 10-20]. Tier 2 (warm, periodic contact): [estimate count and describe]. Current gaps: [expertise or access I lack]. Identify whether my Tier 1 is aligned with my 10-year goals, my biggest network gap, and the highest-leverage relationship to build next.

prompts.name: Network Value Proposition
prompts.template: Define my network value proposition. My expertise: [describe]. My network access: [describe]. What I can offer that most cannot: [describe or help me identify]. Build my give-first strategy for the next 90 days.

prompts.name: Relationship Maintenance System
prompts.template: Design a relationship maintenance system for my network. Tier 1 size: [number]. Tier 2 size: [number]. Time available for relationship maintenance: [hours/week]. Give me a lightweight, systematic touchpoint cadence that maintains capital without becoming a second job.

examples.title: Consultant Building a Referral Network
examples.context: Independent consultant relying on cold outreach for new business
examples.framework_applied: Give First Protocol + Relationship Capital Architecture
examples.intervention: Identified 20 non-competing consultants serving same client profile. Began making introductions between clients and these consultants, sharing relevant research, providing feedback on their content.
examples.outcome: Within 12 months, 60% of new business came from referrals from this network. Cold outreach effectively eliminated.

vocabulary.definition: The accumulated goodwill, trust, and reciprocity stored in a relationship. Built through consistent giving; depleted through extraction.
Acquaintance-level connections. Research shows weak ties deliver more novel information and job opportunities than strong ties.
A person connecting two otherwise disconnected communities. Bridges receive diverse information early and hold structural power.
Cognitive limit of ~150 stable social relationships. Tier 1 (10-20) and Tier 2 (~150) map to this natural architecture.
Networking philosophy of delivering value before making requests. Builds capital and creates reciprocity without transactional dynamics.
A gap between two unconnected network clusters. Filling structural holes by bridging communities creates disproportionate influence.
